ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for keema matar exists; mapped to 'meat curry (dish)' as the closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKM (meat dish, cooked, with vegetables) used as the closest defensible parent match; no specific keema matar code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for keema matar or equivalent minced-meat curry dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of keema matar in Singapore is approximately 300 g, usually served alongside rice or bread such as naan or roti.

nutrient_source.title: Keema Matar (Minced Meat with Peas) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, adapted composition for lamb-based keema matar
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides composition data for cooked minced meat dishes and green pea preparations. Values for keema matar are derived by combining ICMR-NIN data for cooked minced lamb (mutton keema) and cooked green peas in a spiced gravy, weighted at approximately 65% minced lamb and 20% green peas with 15% gravy base (onion, tomato, oil, spices). Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked ground lamb (NDB 17174) and cooked green peas (NDB 11310). The mild-spicy variant does not materially alter macronutrient composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for keema matar in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous mixed meat-and-legume curry dishes. Green peas have a published GI of approximately 48 (low); cooked minced meat contributes negligible glycemic load. The combined dish GI is estimated conservatively at 35, consistent with high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate meat-legume curries documented in Atkinson et al. (2021) and Foster-Powell et al. (2002). GL per 300 g serving = (35 × 6.8 × 3) / 100 = 7.1, rounded to 8.0 accounting for serving variability. Estimation basis clearly stated; confidence is medium.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spice blends, added salt, and restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of keema matar provides approximately 960 mg sodium, representing 40–48% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to 150–200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) driven by high protein, moderate fat, and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Keema matar has a low estimated GI of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 8 per 300 g serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from lamb and cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 300 g serving provides approximately 11.4 g saturated fat and 144 mg cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose lean lamb mince, and balance intake with vegetables and whole grains to stay within AHA saturated fat guidelines of less than 13 g per day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Keema Matar (also spelled Keema Mattar, Qeema Matar, or Kheema Matar) is a classic North Indian dish consisting of finely minced meat — traditionally lamb or mutton, though beef, chicken, or pork variants exist — cooked with green peas (matar) in a richly spiced onion-tomato gravy. The dish is prepared by first sautéing onions, garlic, and ginger in oil until golden, then adding whole and ground spices such as cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, and chilli. The minced meat is browned in the spice base before tomatoes and green peas are added, and the mixture is simmered until the oil separates and the gravy thickens. The result is a semi-dry, intensely flavoured curry with a savoury, mildly spiced profile, tender meat texture, and the slight sweetness of cooked peas. In Singapore, keema matar is served at North Indian restaurants and is typically accompanied by naan, roti, or steamed basmati rice. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with a low glycemic index of approximately 35, making it suitable for blood glucose management. The dish provides significant iron, zinc, and vitamin B12 from lamb, and dietary fibre and vitamin C from green peas. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations and should be monitored by individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The mild-spicy variant uses a reduced quantity of chilli while retaining the full aromatic spice profile.
common_user_queries: Is keema matar good for diabetics?
What meat is used in keema matar?

confidence_reason: No exact keema matar entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked minced lamb and cooked green pea data with weighted composition modelling. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-documented and the adaptation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (2017) cooked minced lamb and green pea data; no single exact entry for keema matar exists in any approved primary source. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is not available. Sodium value reflects typical restaurant preparation and may be higher than home-cooked versions. The mild-spicy descriptor refers to reduced chilli quantity and does not materially alter macronutrient composition. Halal status is assumed based on lamb sourcing at North Indian restaurants in Singapore; individual restaurant certification should be verified. Dietary fibre contribution is primarily from green peas. The dish is naturally gluten-free when served without bread accompaniments.
